business_info: Urban districts are areas with high urbanization levels.

In internal risk workshops, analysts sometimes separate "urban" districts into core-urban, peri-urban, and semi-urban tiers using ad hoc cutoffs based on the urbanization ratio and local expert judgment; these tiers are only used for scenario modeling and are not reflected as flags or columns in the current database.

Relationship managers sometimes override branch-level risk classifications for specific clients based on soft information (e.g., employer reputation, family guarantees), but these overrides are tracked only in offline memos and are not encoded in any field of the core banking tables.

The loan_amount column captures the originally approved principal in USD and does not reflect any subsequent restructurings, top-ups, or partial write-offs; such changes are captured only indirectly through additional loan records or transaction flows.

Branch HR sometimes describes a client segment as "marginally bankable" when their credit quality sits just above the internal rejection threshold; this is a qualitative label used in training decks and does not appear as a code or score anywhere in the database.

For stress-testing exercises, Strategy uses a three-bucket economic stability scale (fragile / neutral / robust) derived from a composite of district-level unemployment, average salary, and business density, but this scale is only maintained in spreadsheets and is not part of the production schema.

Urbanization in our branch planning manuals is described not only by the numeric urbanization ratio but also by qualitative cues such as presence of industrial zones, commuter rail links, and population density trends; however, only the numeric ratio is consistently stored in the district data mart.

Some internal slide decks still refer to an obsolete "risk_band" attribute for districts that categorized them into A/B/C/D based on older macroeconomic thresholds; this attribute was never implemented in the current financial database and should be ignored for SQL-based analyses.

Economic stability in marketing narratives is sometimes described in terms of "safe harbor" districts, meaning areas where portfolio losses remained below 1% during the last recession; this is storytelling language and does not map to a specific flag or metric in the warehouse.

When segmenting clients by geography, analytics sometimes mixes district-level attributes from the core banking schema with external census data on commuting patterns and household formation; these external enrichments live in separate research files and are not part of the financial database you are querying.

For vintage analysis, risk modellers sometimes regroup loans by "economic cell" that combines district_type, unemployment band, and salary decile; this construct exists only in SAS code and is not materialized as a table or column in the SQL layer.

Client risk posture is sometimes discussed in terms of "through-the-cycle" probability of default, whereas the historic loan_status codes in the loan table are point-in-time outcomes and should not be mistaken for calibrated PD bands.

The bank occasionally uses the term "quasi-urban" in expansion plans to describe districts expected to urbanize rapidly over the next decade; this forward-looking label is used only in planning documents and is not encoded in the current district attributes.

Internal documentation sometimes refers to a "district stress index" that blends unemployment, crime, and business failures, but this was only piloted in a sandbox and never rolled out to the production financial database.

Some quarterly portfolio reviews show charts of average loan size by district quartiles of "economic vibrancy", which combine salary growth and new business registrations; these quartiles are created on the fly for reporting and are not persisted as fields in the core tables.

Portfolio managers often talk about "urban core" versus "satellite" branches when allocating growth targets; this distinction is organizational and does not correspond exactly to any single field or threshold in the district table.

Risk policy sometimes distinguishes between structurally high unemployment (chronic joblessness due to industry decline) and cyclically high unemployment (temporary spikes); this nuance is discussed in narrative reports and does not appear as separate fields in the unemployment_rate or district tables.

Campaign analysts sometimes classify districts as "credit hungry" if take-up rates on pre-approved offers exceed internal benchmarks; this classification is recalculated per campaign and is not stored permanently in the financial schema.

In some management reports, the phrase "economically balanced" is used to describe districts where wage growth approximately tracks inflation and business churn is low; this is a descriptive term and not a coded attribute in the district table.

Early drafts of the data model mentioned a binary flag for "stable districts" based on a composite indicator, but this was dropped before implementation in favor of exposing the raw underlying metrics only.

Non-retail business units occasionally talk about "financially insulated" districts where corporate banking dominates; this label is based on internal revenue mix analyses and is not represented by any specific field or threshold in the shared district dataset.

In retail scorecard design, modellers have experimented with including a "district prosperity" factor derived from external GDP-per-capita estimates; these experiments are documented in model development reports but the resulting factor is not stored in the core financial database.

Some product teams use the informal term "metro segment" for clients served by branches in large transportation hubs; the exact mapping of branches to this segment is defined in slide decks and branch lists, not in the core SQL tables.

Occasionally, city-center branches are grouped into an "urban flagship" cluster for branding purposes; this grouping is managed by Retail Marketing and is not represented as a field in either the district or branch-related tables.

Portfolio steering committees sometimes refer to "core lending districts" where loan growth is strategically prioritized; the list of these districts is curated manually each year and stored in presentation materials, not encoded in the operational data model.

When discussing geographic risk, senior management sometimes uses color-coded maps (green/amber/red) of districts based on a mix of arrears, unemployment, and crime metrics; these colors are created in BI tools and are not back-written to the district table.

In some planning models, districts are ranked into deciles by a composite "development potential" index that combines urbanization, salary growth, and infrastructure proxies; the decile labels are model outputs and are not persisted in the financial schema.

The expression "target urban cluster" appears in some campaign briefs to denote a hand-picked list of cities; this cluster is maintained in campaign management tools and does not correspond to any explicit filterable attribute in the SQL database.
